submissions.fileName: Ethics in Food_ Recipe Workplan Template.pdf
Lata DEV_ Food Ethics Workbook - Checkpoint 1.pdf

data.description:
You are required to complete the Safety Task. This is a compulsory task and must be completed in order to demonstrate a satisfactory understanding of Safety in the kitchen. Please see the task sheet attached. Please upload a PDF version of your comic strip to this learning task.
 

Safety in the kitchen

 

  1. Read the following article from Choice about kitchen safety: https://www.choice.com.au/home-and-living/kitchen/food-processors-mixers-and-blenders/articles/cooking-and-kitchen-safety-tips 

 

  1. Choose one of the following kitchen tasks:

  • Using a knife

  • Using a stove to boil water or fry with oil

  • Using an oven

  • Cooking with chicken

 

  1. Using Canva https://www.canva.com/create/comic-strips/  (or similar software of your choice), create a comic strip to show:

 

  • Potential safety issues with that task (what could go wrong?)

  • Safe practices to perform that task to reduce the risk (how to prevent it going wrong)

 

There is no need to log in and you should only use the free images and graphics. You can download the free watermarked draft for submission.
data.name: Safety and Hygiene Comic Strip

data.description:
In this task, students will delve into the ethical considerations surrounding food consumption. A topic will be chosen from a diverse range of options such as vegetarianism, food security, or fair-trade, and embark on a journey of discovery. Through research, reflection, and creative expression, you will explore the complexities of ethical food choices. This project will challenge you to critically analyse the impact of your dietary decisions on society, the environment, and personal well-being. Students will engage with the fascinating world of ethical food consumption and make meaningful connections to their own life and values.

The theory grade for this CAT will consist of a combined score from two tasks. The practical grade for this CAT will be based on a dish that is selected and prepared by the student.
data.name: Sem 1 - CAT: Ethics in food
data.taskReportDescription: Students will explore ethical food consumption topics like vegetarianism or fair-trade, analysing their impact on society, the environment, and personal wellbeing, fostering connections to their own values. They will create a zine and practical dish for their chosen topic.

data.description:
Students recieve a case study related to budgeting where they they write a design brief and design a meal. They plan all components of the production including writing a recipe, ordering the ingredients required and the final plating and presentation. Students will prepare the meal in class as a practical assessment.

This task requires students to be organised and meet various deadlines. Students will be required to submit the following:
  • Food order - Google form submitted via google classroom. If a students misses this due date, food will not be ordered for them and they will therefore receive a Not Satisfactory for the production grade.
  • A budgeting production plan CAT booklet has been printed for each student and they will need to complete this in class. This includes sections on planning, justification and reflection.
data.name: Sem 1 - CAT: Budgeting

results.result: Lara, you demonstrated your practical skills well during this practical assessment, producing a good finished serve and a well organised approach to the task. Your workplan contained information on all required fields and contained a lot of detail to help ensure your dish went smoothly. Your dish was well-presented, with good attention to detail in plating and portioning. A little more refinement would take it to an even higher level. Well done!

data.taskReportDescription: Students designed and planned a meal under budgeting constraints in response to a case study. Students prepared the meal in class as a practical assessment.

data.description:
This learning task is for students to submit the first checkpoint for their CAT: Ethics in food. This is a hurdle requirement for this CAT in order to be able to complete checkpoint two and checkpoint three. 

CHECKPOINT 1 - Define, Discover, Develop
Task components: Data Collection Table, Workplan, Recipe, Justification of Recipe and Referencing.
data.name: Sem 1 - DEV: Ethics in Food Checkpoint One

results.result: Your data collection table is clear and mostly complete, though a little more detail would strengthen the quality of your research. I look forward to seeing what you produce in the practical lesson, as well as how you present your research in the Zine you are going to make!

data.description: Students complete a topic test which demonstrates their knowledge of key science skills, memory theories and Performance Psychology.

data.description: Students complete some practice at writing and analysing hypotheses.
data.name: Hypothesis Writing Sheet

data.description: Students conduct and write up an experiment demonstrating key elements of memory theory, as well as demonstrating knowledge and understanding of key science skills.
data.name: Sem 1 - CDT: Practical Application of Psychological Theory

results.result:
Well done on completing your CDT. This was important practice in the lead up to your End of Semester Psychology test CAT and will be important for you to look carefully at the feedback. Your response to the extraneous variables question was strong. In terms of some key areas I would like you to reflect on and revise prior to our CAT:
- Your hypothesis needs to focus less on the theory and more on the practical experiment undertaken. You especially need to think about how we are measuring the variables. Please look at the comments on your hypothesis and speak to me if you're not sure about any of this. 

Well done on a very sound result.

data.description: Students complete a quiz to demonstrate understanding in various areas of specialisation in Psychology and Psychology as a profession, as well as demonstrating knowledge and understanding of key science skills.
data.name: Sem 1 - CDT: Psychology as a Profession and Research Methods

results.result:
Well done on Completing your first Psychology assessment Lara. Overall a great result, well done. You were confident in identifying your variables, writing your hypothesis, and the aim of the research, well done as these are important areas of focus for our upcoming CAT. 
To improve there were some errors in your multiple choice section that I would like you to particularly reflect on: 
- Within vs between groups design (why would we use one over the other)
- Ethical guidelines

data.description: Students watch the feature film Titanic and complete a written investigation into the use of Production and Story Elements within key scenes. Detailed analysis of aspects of film making such as editing, lighting, cinematography and point of view are discussed.
data.name: Sem 1 - CAT: Narrative Film Analysis

results.result: You give a clear and relevant discussion of Titanicâs camera angles and point of view Lara. You identify key features of costumes and set design and make some connection to historical authenticity and narrative purpose. Your analysis of editing is competent, noting its role in pacing and scene transitions. You discuss sound with an understanding of its contribution to atmosphere and audience response. Some film terminology is used appropriately, though the depth of explanation could be expanded.

data.name: Sem 1 - CAT: Production

results.result: You completed all production tasks to a high standard and demonstrated a solid understanding of media production processes. Your green screen sequence successfully incorporated keying effects and showed good attention to visual continuity. The cereal box design displayed strong design skills, including effective use of colour, layout, imagery, and persuasive advertising techniques. Your free choice short film communicated its intended message clearly and featured competent filming and editing techniques that engaged the audience.

data.name: Dev Task - Green Screen

results.result: Youâve demonstrated a confident understanding of green screen production and digital editing techniques. The composition is clear, and your lighting setup effectively separates the subject from the screen, resulting in a strong key. The backgrounds are relevant and thoughtfully matched to the action, while the pacing and structure of your edit show good planning. The audio design ties the sequence together well. For further improvement, focus on refining edge details and ensuring lighting and colour tones remain consistent throughout the sequence.

data.description: Students complete a practice test to demonstrate their understanding of validity, repeatability and reproducibility.
data.name: Sem 1 - CDT: Designing and Evaluating a Scientific Investigation

results.result:
Areas of strength shown in the task:
- clearly and concisely stating the aim from information given about an experiment. 
- interpretation of experimental results to justify with sound reasoning the validity of that experiment. 
- accurately identifying the best suitable graph type to display the results from a given table of results and drawing it accurately. 

Areas for improvement:
- interpretation of experimental results to justify with sound reasoning the reliability (repeatability and reproducibility) of that experiment. 
- recognising reasonable improvements that can be made to an experimental method to ensure the data collected from the experiment is reliable. 

A suggested strategy for future learning:
- applying scientific skills and knowledge in a more complex and in-depth nature when analysing and interpreting scientific data.

data.description: Students will investigate a case study of a patient experiencing symptoms to determine whether they have Hoffman Syndrome, Kocher–Debré–Semelaigne Syndrome, or Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy. The task requires students to compare symptoms, reference reliable sources, consider ethical aspects in healthcare, assess website credibility, explore endocrine system feedback loops
data.name: Sem 1 - CDT:  Body Systems Research Task

results.result:
Areas of strength shown in the task:
- ensuring to list all symptoms when preparing a symptom list of a particular condition. 
- ability to correctly diagnose a patient with a medical condition by thoroughly researching all possible medical conditions. 
- providing at least three credible source references to websites that were used during research of the medical condition. 

Areas for improvement:
- using the patient profile as evidence to contradict a particular medical condition by listing all patient symptoms that are not related to the condition. 
- giving detailed and specific information, ensuring the question has been fully answered. 

A suggested strategy for future learning:
- developing good time management to stay on track with research information and meeting due dates.

I encourage you to share your opinions and insights more in class discussions.

data.taskReportDescription:
The Year 9 curriculum supports students to critique the effectiveness of strategies to enhance health, fitness and wellbeing. To demonstrate this knowledge in a written test, students will use their practical experience and fitness and training concepts learnt throughout Term 2.

Â
data.taskTitleOnReport: Sem 1 - CAT: Fitness and Training Test

data.description:
From Levels 9-10 in the Vic curriculum, students recognize, develop and implement strategies in order to work effectively in groups and identify ways to be proactive in initiating strategies to prevent and/or accomplish positive resolutions to conflict. They need to evaluate their own and others contribution to group tasks, critiquing roles including leadership and provide useful feedback to peers, evaluate task outcomes and make recommendations for improvements in relation to team goals. They also need to work towards demonstrating their ability to acknowledging the importance of empathy and the acceptance of diversity.

This is completed for both practical and theoretical classes.
data.name: Sem 1 - CAT: Year 9 Social Capabilities

data.description: In this subject, students learn to apply more specialised movement skills and complex movement strategies and concepts in different movement environments. They also explore movement concepts and strategies to evaluate and refine their own and others’ movement performances. Students analyse how participation in physical activity and sport influence an individual’s health and wellbeing and explores role participation.

gradingItems.name: Overall Movement and Physical Activity Assessment

data.name: Sem 1 - CAT: Year 9 Movement and Physical Activity

results.result:
Lara, youâve shown a basic understanding of the skills and strategies in our Minor Games unit and are participating at a competent level. You demonstrate a willingness to join in and support your team but tend to take a backseat during group activities.

To develop further, focus on increasing your involvement during games, asking questions when unsure, and contributing to team discussions. Practising your communication and decision-making will help build your confidence and teamwork skills.

You're on the right track, keep working at it!

data.description:
CAT Length: 40 minutes writing time
Details:
  • Individual task to be completed in test conditions
  • Technology free (no calculators)
  • No summary notes are to be used
  • There is no formula sheet provided in the task
CAT Content:
The CAT may include multiple choice and short answer problems on the following content covered in Chapter 1.2 to 1.9 – Indices and Surds of our textbook as well as rationalizing the denominator. 
  • Ch. 1.2: Real Numbers
  • Ch. 1.3: Rounding significant figures
  • Ch. 1.4: Review of index laws
  • Ch. 1.5: Raising Powers
  • Ch. 1.6 Negative Indices
  • Ch. 1.7: Scientific notation
  • Ch. 1.8: Square roots and Cube roots
  • Ch. 1.9: Simplifying surds (and rationalising the denominator, see handout)

Revision Material:
  • Students are expected to complete all of the classwork on the chapter content listed above in order to prepare for the task.
  • A practice test with solutions are provided to students as an additional revision resource.
data.name: Sem 1 - CAT 1: Number and Indices Skills (Tech-Free)

results.result:
Areas of strength shown in the task:
- rounding numbers to a specified number of decimal places. 
- writing a numeral, to a given number of significant figures, in scientific notation. 
- using surds to evaluate exact answers to worded problems. 

Areas for improvement:
- simplifying expressions involving surds. 
- simplifying complex algebraic expressions involving negative indices using the seventh exponent law. 

A suggested strategy for future learning:
- during the assessment, read each question a total of three times, this helps to minimise careless errors. Read the whole assessment first. Determine the best order to complete the questions in, ideally easiest to hardest. Read the question again before answering it. After a solution has been written, re-read the question to ensure the written solution fully answers the question.

data.name: Sem 1 - CAT 2: Measurement and Pythagoras Application (Tech-Access)

results.result:
Areas of strength shown in the task:
- determining the area of a given shape using the appropriate formula. 
- converting between units of volume and units of capacity. 
- using the known area of a shape to find a side length.

Areas for improvement:
- calculating the total surface area of a cylinder. 
- identifying a sector and finding its area. 

A suggested strategy for future learning:
- Remember that details are important in solving an application question. Ensure to define variables, draw diagrams if appropriate and then select an appropriate response to justify this selection. Check the question posed has been fully answered. Listen and follow the advice the teacher has given throughout the year to optimise your knowledge and learning.

data.description: Students will craft their own creative piece exploring a gap or silence in one of the dystopian texts they have studied in class. This gap/silence may be a new perspective, unheard or silenced voice. This may include a new beginning, new event or an alternate/extended ending. The creative response must remain faithful to aspects of the dystopian genre, themes and features studied. The emphasis in this task is for students to develop their creative writing skills in a more focused way. Thus creativity is marked in this task and scaffolded throughout the drafting process.

gradingItems.name: VCERV: English - English Reading and Viewing

data.name: Sem 1- CAT: Dystopian Creative Writing Task

results.result:
Well done on presenting an incredibly well written short story Lara. Your written expression is very clear, and the interior monologue of your main character created a strong atmosphere of fear and dread that connects well to your overall purpose. The dialogue also supported this well, and while the final paragraph felt rushed, the structure was used to constantly engage your reader. 

While your message was powerful and relevant to our real world, there was little connection to dystopian themes . The ideas around gender inequality were contained to this relationship, they were not reflected in the broader society and the world of the text. This theme would have been great to explore on a larger level, as this would have aligned your piece with dystopian ideas.

data.taskTitleOnReport: Sem 1- CAT: Dystopian Creative Writing Task

data.name: Developmental Task - Build Your Own Dystopia

results.result:
Well done on your analysis of this shot Lara. You're mostly able to identify the literal film techniques present; you're descriptive adjectives used to discuss the acting choices were especially great. When interpreting their symbolic meaning however, you need to connect them to various themes from the film; for example, how does the natural lighting of the shot reflect Christof's desire to maintain control of Truman's life through the presentation of a false reality?

It was great to see that you're able to think about the directorial intent. Try to more specifically connect this to the themes from the film we've discussed, and always make sure that you are writing formally (Don't include opinions like 'famous line' for example)

gradingItems.name: VCERV: English - English Reading and Viewing

data.name: Sem 1- CAT: 'The Truman Show'  Analysis

results.result: Thank you for completing this still analysis for The Truman Show Lara. Well done identifying what film techniques were present within the still. Your connection of the plot elements (Such as Trumanâs trauma) to the symbolic nature of darkness was fantastic. However, there was very little explanation of what these film techniques actually meant beyond just identifying them. What was Weir suggesting through each one? Going forward, please also try to write more formally, and don't sign post your discussion so overly

data.taskReportDescription:
Students will view the film 'The Truman Show' and explore themes, characters, plot and the construction of film as text, in particular the importance of mise-en-scene. Once students have completed this in-depth study, they will be required to examine a series of still shots and analyse these in accordance to their study of film components from The Truman Show by producing a written analysis.Â

data.description:
Students create a research report outlining food security levels in Melbourne and strategies to address causes of future food insecurity.

Support resources (CAT research):
  • Overview of food security solutions
  • OzHarvest - food accessibility
  • Food waste - management strategies
  • Farmland and urbanisation (urban sprawl)
  • Food supply chain - resilience, planning
  • Water usage - recycled water, desalination
data.name: Sem 1 - CAT: Food Security (Geography)
data.taskReportDescription: Students create a research report outlining food security levels in Melbourne and strategies to address causes of future food insecurity.

data.description: Students investigate a case study about a modern business to examine the economic impact of technological advancements to consumers and producers.
data.name: Sem 1 - CDT: Case Study (Economics)

results.result:
You demonstrated a solid understanding of how Uber Eats benefited during COVID and clearly explained how restaurants adapted their business operations, achieving full marks for Question 1. Your responses showed a good understanding of key business challenges and impacts, and you were able to identify relevant issues affecting Uber Eatsâ future success. You also attempted to justify which problem was the most urgent, showing developing analytical and evaluative skills. Overall, your work showed good effort and an ability to apply Economics and Business concepts to a real-world case study.

To improve, focus on developing your explanations in greater detail and using more direct evidence from the case study to support your ideas. In Questions 2, 3, and 4, some responses needed stronger analysis of how the problems and impacts would affect Uber Eats specifically. When justifying your opinion, try to compare the issues more clearly and explain why one problem is more significant than the other. Adding more detailed reasoning and consistently linking your answers back to the question will help you achieve higher marks in future Economics tasks.

data.description: In this assessment, students answer the question 'will Melbourne be food secure in 2050?' to demonstrate their geographic skills (data interpretation) and knowledge (food security, biomes). The students are provided a 'data booklet' of research to interpret and support their response to the inquiry question.
data.name: Sem 1 - CDT: Data Interpretation (Geography)

results.result:
Areas for improvement: 
- Data booklet annotations: Extensive annotations of data sources, however, need to expand upon observations with in-depth analysis (not just âwhatâ this data shows but âwhyâ this is significant). 
- PQE - Map: Response uses some elements of PQE to describe general patterns/trends in data source, however, specific detail is required (statistics, location names, dates). 
- PQE- Graph: Response uses some elements of PQE to describe general patterns/trends in data source, however, specific detail is required (statistics, location names, dates). 

Strengths: 
- Melbourne food security table: Table includes detailed information relevant to food security levels in Melbourne, categorised into strengths/weaknesses and geographical factors (e.g. SHEEPT, SPICESS).
- IDEAL (3):  Response effectively uses IDEAL template to explain and outline a conclusion of food security levels in Melbourne.

data.description: One person per team should upload their presentation onto this learning task

data.description:
Students demonstrate their understanding of the features of an effective small business by creating a new business for the Kingsway, Glen Waverley shopping district.

Students 'present' their business in a 60 second 'elevator pitch' to persuade potential investors to support their business. Students are assessed on a 'High - Medium - Low' grading scale based on their persuasive language, entrepreneurship (creativity of business concept), and communication skills (engage, inform).
data.name: Sem 2 Learning Task - Build-A-Business
